news_college_hockey.title: Here's how this year's NCAA Tournament would have looked under my proposed 24-team, "super regional" alignment
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Last week at the annual American Hockey Coaches Association convention in Florida, there was inevitable discussion about the NCAA Tournament. We had details of what was discussed during the convention — from the tournament to potential rule changes — in a newsletter last week:

The idea of expanding the NCAA Tournament beyond its current 16-team bracket was discussed, and I’m told there are several coaches in favor of expanding. Multiple sources said that the idea of expanding the tournament had more support than the idea of going to on-campus sites for the first two rounds before advancing to a neutral-site Frozen Four.

There are still logistical hurdles to overcome. The NCAA typically has used a benchmark of 25 percent of participating teams qualifying for the NCAA Tournament. Division I men’s hockey is already at that benchmark (currently 25.4 percent).

Today’s newsletter looks at my proposed 24-team tournament, and how it would have looked last season.

Also in today’s newsletter:

  • Future sites for the Frozen Four and Regional rounds of the NCAA Tournament are expected in the coming months, with locations for the 2029, 2030, and 2031 tournaments.

  • We have some notes on the requirements of NCAA regional hosts.

  • Stonehill is moving forward with its plans for an on-campus rink.

  • There were WHL teams who reportedly passed on players who were “too good” because they didn’t want to lose those players at 18 years old to the pros or NCAA.

  • Gavin McKenna’s mother was charged with furnishing liquor to minors the same night that Gavin McKenna was charged with assault.

This is issue #677 of the College Hockey Insider newsletter.

news_college_hockey.title: Who could be the next Harvard head coach?
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Ted Donato is stepping down as Harvard’s head coach at the end of the academic year, the program announced Thursday afternoon. The Crimson said they will begin a national search for a new head coach immediately.

“It is with immense gratitude that I have decided to end my tenure at Harvard as head coach of the Men’s Hockey program,” Donato said. “I am proud and grateful for all the incredible moments, everlasting memories, and relationships from my 27 years connected to the University as a student-athlete, coach, and parent.

“I feel truly blessed to have had the opportunity to work with such talented and driven student-athletes and staff of the highest character. With the ever-changing landscape of college athletics, I believe it is the right time to pass the torch to the next leader of the Harvard Men’s Hockey program.”

Rumblings about Donato’s departure had been circulating among coaches and agents for nearly two weeks. On April 25, College Hockey Insider first heard that Donato had been in conversations with NHL teams about potential scouting roles.

Despite multiple coaches and agents “hearing” that he was leaving Harvard, they were all second or third-hand accounts, and we couldn’t independently verify the information.

There were other signs. Most notably, assistant coach James Marcou took the open assistant position at UMass. Harvard’s other assistant, Matt Gilroy, is reportedly in the mix for another job as well. Sources in the coaching industry considered it “telling” that Harvard had not moved to replace Marcou. Many believed it validated the growing sense that Donato was on his way out.

Now we know why there wasn’t much movement on replacing Marcou.

It’s possible Harvard began conducting due diligence on potential candidates over the past two weeks, which could mean this hiring process moves quickly. Given where we are on the calendar, that wouldn’t be surprising in the least.

Harvard has a rich list of hockey alumni. Not many have gone into coaching, but several have carved out prominent roles in NHL front offices. Those connections could open the door to some outside-the-box candidates.

Here’s a look at 18 candidates who could be next in line to lead the Crimson:

news_college_hockey.title: Here's what was discussed last week at the annual AHCA coaches' convention
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
College hockey coaches and power brokers met late last week in Bonita Springs, Florida, for the annual American Hockey Coaches Association convention.

Several key topics were discussed, and today’s newsletter is packed with details, including:

— Extensive discussion on how the NCAA’s age-based eligibility proposal will impact the sport, including a meeting with the NCAA’s chief legal officer on the issue.

— The NCAA’s new tampering proposal.

— The future of roster construction in the era of roster caps and age-based eligibility.

— Potential rule changes under consideration, along with a timeline for implementation this offseason.

— Continued debate surrounding NCAA regionals, including another push for home sites, a radical proposal for implementing them, tournament expansion, and the possibility of teams bidding to host.

— Expanding the regular-season schedule and recruiting calendar.

Also in today’s issue:

  • Is Landon Dupont playing college hockey next season? And is Michigan the frontrunner?

  • A bunch of coaching news on former NCAA head coaches losing pro jobs, assistant openings/hirings, and more.

  • UNH athletic Alison Rich is out at the end of the year.

  • The USHL signed Glenn Hefferan to an extension.

  • How much does the NCHC make off of NCHC.tv?

news_college_hockey.title: The NCAA is trying to push through the five-year eligibility rule next month
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
The NCAA could push its five-year eligibility proposal through the legislative process next month by categorizing the change as “emergency legislation.” What would that mean for current and future college hockey players?

Plus, a look inside what some players are being told about a fifth year — and their potential options when it comes to entering the portal.

Also in today’s issue:

  • The latest from the transfer portal includes the official entry of Maine forward Justin Poirier. Plus, BU added two defensemen, and one of the top offensive defensemen in the portal committed to Lake Superior.

  • The NCAA is also considering a “legal tampering period” when it comes to the transfer portal.

  • Michigan coach Brandon Naurato is headed to the World Championships.

  • A note on a potential candidate for Jeff Hamilton’s staff at Yale.

This is issue #674 of the College Hockey Insider newsletter.

You can also find me on X at @MikeMcMahonCHN.

The NCAA is trying to push through the five-year eligibility rule next month

NCAA president Charlie Baker is pushing for the Division I cabinet to vote on the five-year eligibility rule next month as emergency legislation. If it’s approved, he wants it adopted in June.

But that’s not all.

As part of the emergency legislation, policies regarding professional involvement — both pre- and post-enrollment — are also being reviewed and could be changed.

The proposal appears to have broad support among power brokers in college athletics. The idea of the “five-in-five” model was also included in President Donald Trump’s executive order on college sports released earlier this month.

“I have had a lot of people offer me thoughts about implementation, but I haven’t had anybody say to me that this is a bad idea,” Baker said. “It makes it harder for somebody to show up at the age of 19 or 20 or 21 and claim to be a freshman after playing three years of professional sports somewhere else.”

news_college_hockey.title: How the CHL rule change might impact college hockey's recruiting timeline
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Everyone understands the impact of last fall’s NCAA rule change regarding CHL players. With players from the OHL, WHL, and QMJHL now eligible to play NCAA hockey, the second wave of former CHL players is set to arrive on campus this fall.

The rule is also reshaping how NCAA programs approach recruiting. In fact, some coaches would like to see the timeline pushed back in an effort to standardize the recruitment of older (i.e. junior-aged) players.

Also in today’s newsletter:

  • A full recap of today’s transactions in the transfer portal, including four commitments (to Stonehill, Vermont, and Maine) along with Adam Barone’s entry.

  • Princeton endowed its head coaching position thanks to a donation from a group of alums.

  • Omaha has re-upped its contract with Learfield.

How the CHL rule change might impact college hockey’s recruiting timeline

There have been plenty of ripple effects from the NCAA’s rule change on CHL players, which was instituted a little more than a year ago.

One area that hasn’t been discussed much yet is how the change might impact the college hockey recruiting calendar.

Programs are now recruiting players further along in their development than they were 18 months ago. In fact, some coaches would like to see changes to the recruiting calendar because of it.

You can also find me on X at @MikeMcMahonCHN.

Yale officially hires Jeff Hamilton as its new head coach

Yale officially named Jeff Hamilton as its new head coach on Monday.

Hamilton is a Yale alum who was backed by several prominent alumni during the interview process. He had a long NHL career and has recently been coaching at the youth level in Connecticut. He’s a former Yale captain and remains the program’s all-time leading scorer.

Hamilton owns the Connecticut Junior Rangers.

“I’m honored and humbled to lead the next era of Yale men’s hockey,” Hamilton said in a release. “Thank you to Vicky [Chun], Danielle Upham, and the athletics administrative team for giving my family and me the opportunity to return to a place that has had such a profound impact on our lives,” said Hamilton.

“Building on the foundation laid by my greatest mentor, Coach Tim Taylor, and on the extraordinary success of the past decades, we will pursue excellence with the highest expectations. My staff and I are committed to elevating the student-athlete experience to the highest standard of recruiting, developing, and championing the very best in college hockey, while providing opportunities that are uniquely Yale.”

news_college_hockey.title: 10 thoughts on the transfer portal after one week
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
The transfer portal has been open for almost one week, and there are exactly the same number of entries six days into the window as there were in the entire last cycle (308 players). Of those players, 147 (47.7 percent) have already announced commitments for next season.

Today’s newsletter starts with 10 thoughts on the transfer portal as we approach the halfway point of the entry window. Players have until April 27 to enter the portal. After that, they can only enter if their financial aid is cut or if their program undergoes a head coaching change.

Also in today’s issue:

  • Yale is getting close to hiring a new head coach, and it appears that alum Jeff Hamilton is the favorite. Where do things stand? Who is running the search? We have some info.

  • ESPN drew record numbers for the Frozen Four and the national title game last week. Plus, notes on the production elements behind the broadcast, and more.

  • In our notebook, Elliotte Friedman reports that David Carle turned down $25 million from the Chicago Blackhawks last year, BC flipped a goalie commitment, Yost Ice Arena suffered some storm damage, notes on future Frozen Four sites, college hockey expansion, and more.

This is issue #670 of the College Hockey Insider newsletter.

Thanks for reading and being part of this community — your support truly means everything. Whether you’ve been here from the start or just joined us, we’re grateful to have you along for the ride.

If you have ideas for a future newsletter, tips to pass along, or questions for a mailbag, I’d love to hear from you. You can reach me anytime at mike@collegehockeyinsider.com. Sponsorship inquiries are also welcome.

You can also find me on X at @MikeMcMahonCHN.

10 thoughts on the transfer portal after one week

(1) I wrote about this earlier in the week, but money is playing a bigger role in this portal cycle than any we’ve seen.

Multiple players (at least three that I know of) have told teams they were committed, only to de-commit hours later when a bigger-money offer came along. Multiple coaches told me last week that conversations with players or agents have started with, “Team X is offering $30,000.”

And as you would expect, there are a lot of “backroom deals” happening. But that has always happened. You’re never going to stop that. Several players have committed to a new school within hours of entering the portal.

news_college_hockey.title: Tuesday transfer portal thoughts
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
The first day of the 2026 transfer portal is finally in the books — and it delivered plenty of chaos.

In total, 273 players have entered the portal, easily the highest number ever recorded on opening day (some of those were pre-entries as grad students or coaching changes). For context, last year’s full cycle produced 308 entries, which itself set a record.

Here’s how the numbers stack up year by year:

2026 (ongoing): 273
2025: 308
2024: 294
2023: 293
2022: 247

I’ll have a more complete breakdown in a later newsletter, but with the pace of movement already this brisk, it felt necessary to get some early thoughts on paper this Tuesday morning.

In the meantime, subscribers can access the full portal tracker, with complete with rankings based on 2025–26 performance and per-60 analytics from InStat (link below the paywall), while the free page continues to update with daily entries and commitments.

A few takeaways from Day 1:

— The early surge is no surprise. This is the first year operating under a shortened transfer window, trimmed to just 15 days. In previous seasons, players had up to 40 days — and in some cases nearly two months — to make a decision. With less time to weigh options, the urgency to enter early has intensified, leading to a front-loaded wave of movement.

Historically, most entries came within the first three to five days. That trend may now be compressed even further.

Read more

news_college_hockey.title: Monday Morning Portal Update
news_college_hockey.author: Mike McMahon
news_college_hockey.summary:
Without a doubt, this is the wildest first day of the transfer portal in its short history.

It’s 12:47 p.m. as I write this, and we’re already up to 244 entries. Last season, there were 308 total entries.

Some of that is because the window is shorter this year, so more players are jumping in right as it opens. But still, this is as hectic as we’ve ever seen it.

For now, I have Evan Murr as the top player entering the portal. Sources said he’s going to Minnesota. Other top players include Justin Poirier (Maine), Jack Stockfish (Holy Cross), and Ben Muthersbaugh (Union).

Arizona State has not entered any players into the portal as I write this. The compliance departments at the individual schools are actually the ones who enter players into the portal. However, I’m told there will be several Sun Devils entering, including Cullen Potter (who is headed to Michigan State), Brasen Boser, and Sam Court.

If you want to follow along with the entries and commitments (and you aren’t a paid newsletter subscriber), my free tracker page is here:

However, paid newsletter subscribers get access to my full tracker sheet, which I am trying to stay on top of today, but entries are coming in faster than I can update the sheet. All of the names are on there, but the stats will be input throughout the day today.

My full tracker sheet ranks players using their in-season stats plus situational analytics (per 60). It also has numbers on total entries, total commits, commit percentage, and more.

Paid newsletter subscribers will have the link below. If you want to sign up to become a paid subscriber, you can also do so by clicking below.

I’m expecting my entire day to be occupied filling up the portal tracker and sheet.

Read more

news_national.title: Watch: Macron interrupts speaker to tell audience to be quiet
news_national.summary: The French president stood up during a conference in Kenya to tell the audience to quieten down, saying it was “impossible” for speakers to be heard.

news_national.title: Trump says Iran ceasefire is on 'massive life support'
news_national.summary: The US president criticises an Iranian counteroffer to end the war, and says the month-long ceasefire is "unbelievably weak".

news_national.title: Pupils hopeless and crying after 'poorly worded' Higher Maths exam
news_national.summary: More than 11,000 people have signed a petition calling for a review of the exam saying it was "totally unrecognisable" from what they had prepared for.

news_national.title: Campbell's Bluebird back on Coniston Water almost 60 years after fatal record attempt
news_national.summary: The restored hydroplane returns to the lake where its record-breaking pilot was killed in 1967.

news_national.title: Wordle to become TV quiz show with Savannah Guthrie as host
news_national.summary: The online word game from the New York Times will hit the small screen in the UK and US from next year.

news_national.title: EU agrees sanctions on Israeli settlers over West Bank violence
news_national.summary: The EU's foreign policy chief said "extremism and violence carry consequences", while Israel branded the move "arbitrary".

news_national.title: Man accused of Starmer arson attacks did not intend to endanger life, court hears
news_national.summary: A Ukrainian man accused of the attack says he was forced to start the fire after being recruited online.

news_national.title: Welfare bill will not be included in government's King's Speech
news_national.summary: It is understood that the government is not yet ready to propose a new parliamentary bill on welfare.

news_national.title: Elon Musk and Tim Cook among CEOs expected to accompany Trump on China trip
news_national.summary: A total of 17 US executives are set to join the president on his visit, where he will meet his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping.

news_national.title: Hantavirus outbreak: Who is most at risk of catching the deadly virus?
news_national.author: Alix Martichoux
news_national.summary: While hantavirus respiratory infections may still be rare in the U.S., they can be incredibly deadly.

news_national.title: Watchdog group asks Transportation IG to probe Duffy’s ‘road trip’ with family
news_national.author: Ashleigh Fields
news_national.summary: A watchdog group on Monday asked the Department of Transportation (DOT) Inspector General to probe Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy’s participation in the reality series entitled, “The Great American Road Trip.” The group, Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), urged the IG to investigate who paid for Duffy and his family’s participation in the…

news_national.title: Hegseth’s latest attack on Kelly underlines alarm over US weapons stockpiles
news_national.author: Filip Timotija
news_national.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s latest attack against Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) is underscoring the alarm over the state of the U.S. military’s weapons stockpiles more than two months into the war with Iran.  Hegseth accused Kelly, a Navy veteran and a member of the Senate Armed Services Committee (SASC), of divulging classified information regarding key U.S. munitions…

news_national.title: Trump nominates Kari Lake as US ambassador to Jamaica
news_national.author: Ashleigh Fields
news_national.summary: President Trump on Monday nominated Kari Lake to serve as the US ambassador to Jamaica following her tumultuous tenure at the helm of the U.S. Agency for Global Media (USAGM).  Lake’s appointment was first announced in a White House press release alongside multiple other new nominations.  If confirmed by the Senate, she will replace Scott…

news_national.title: The Memo: Iran ceasefire teeters as Trump blasts proposal from Tehran
news_national.author: Niall Stanage
news_national.summary: The chances of an enduring solution to the 10-week war on Iran look to be growing slimmer by the moment — even as the idea of a return to all-out combat comes with real dangers for President Trump and the wider world. Trump on Monday lambasted Iran’s latest peace proposal as a “piece of garbage.”…

news_national.title: Democrats urge Comer to subpoena prediction markets over suspicious trades
news_national.author: Max Rego
news_national.summary: A group of seven Democrats sent a letter on Monday to House Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chair James Comer (R-K.Y.), asking him to issue subpoenas pursuant to an investigation into “suspicious” trades on prediction markets relating to the war with Iran.  Democratic Reps. Maggie Goodlander (N.H.), Sara Jacobs (Calif.), Seth Magaziner (R.I.), Seth Moulton…

news_national.title: Virginia asks Supreme Court to restore Democrats’ congressional map
news_national.author: Zach Schonfeld
news_national.summary: Virginia Democrats asked the Supreme Court on Monday to restore their new congressional map that would give the party up to four House pickup opportunities in the midterms.  In an emergency appeal, state officials insisted Virginia’s top court committed “judicial defiance” by blocking the map last week. The court ruled Democrats hadn’t followed the proper procedures before voters approved the new congressional lines in an April…

news_national.title: Jimmy Kimmel will air rerun opposite Stephen Colbert’s ‘Late Show’ finale
news_national.author: Teddy Grant
news_national.summary: Kimmel aired a rerun on David Letterman’s last show on “The Late Show" in 2015.

news_national.title: Terms on Trump Mobile site indicate that phones may never come
news_national.author: Michael Bartiromo
news_national.summary: A gold-colored mobile phone bearing President Donald Trump’s name was announced in June 2025. But customers may never see it, according to the website.

news_national.title: Fetterman says WHCA dinner shooting ‘not staged’ as poll shows 1 in 3 Democrats think it was
news_national.author: Ashleigh Fields
news_national.summary: Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Monday shamed his party following a statistic that showed 1 in 3 Democrats believe the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner shooting was staged. “Assassinations + political violence are real. My party can’t be the tin foil hat brigade,” Fetterman wrote in a post on X. “I was there a table…

news_national.title: Sports-mad Portland savors its second chance to root for women’s basketball
news_national.author: Casey Parks
news_national.summary: It’s been nearly a quarter of a century since the city had a WNBA team, and fans showed their support by turning out in record numbers.

news_national.title: Abraham Foxman, influential leader of Anti-Defamation League, dies at 86
news_national.author: Fred A. Bernstein
news_national.summary: As the ADL’s national director for nearly 30 years, he battled antisemitism and other forms of prejudice, building the group into a powerful watchdog.

news_national.title: N.J. World Cup games will be in a county that bans most shopping on Sundays
news_national.author: Tim Craig
news_national.summary: There’s a divisive court battle happening in Bergen County, New Jersey, over 1700s-era blue laws, which limit sales on clothes, home decor and more.

news_national.title: Fulfilling a lifelong dream, 72-year-old will graduate from medical school
news_national.author: Kyle Melnick
news_national.summary: Dawn Zuidgeest-Craft, who worked as a nurse practitioner for four decades while raising four kids, will start a three-year residency in July.

news_national.title: Eight children mourned at mass funeral that captured national attention
news_national.author: Molly Hennessy-Fiske
news_national.summary: A funeral for eight children killed in a mass shooting drew hundreds of mourners, including former congresswoman Gabby Giffords.

news_ktn.title: News Alert: Supreme Court allows Alabama to eliminate congressional district held by a Black Democrat
news_ktn.author: bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-f02df7152f=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com (bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-f02df7152f=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com)
news_ktn.summary:
The Supreme Court’s conservative majority on Monday allowed Alabama to revert to a congressional map with one majority-Black district in a sudden ruling that drew a dissent from the court’s three liberal justices.
 

Monday, May 11, 2026

View in browser 
 

News Alert: Supreme Court allows Alabama to eliminate congressional district held by a Black Democrat

The Supreme Court’s conservative majority on Monday allowed Alabama to revert to a congressional map with one majority-Black district in a sudden ruling that drew a dissent from the court’s three liberal justices.

State officials in Alabama had rushed up to the court late Friday asking it to halt a lower court ruling that has blocked it from using a map it enacted in 2023. It did so based on the court’s blockbuster decision in late April dealing with Louisiana’s congressional map that severely weakened the scope of the landmark Voting Rights Act of 1965.

Read more

news_ktn.title: Breaking News: Virginia Democrats make emergency appeal to U.S. Supreme Court to save new House maps that would give party a boost
news_ktn.author: 0101019e18f0ccf6-397742dd-4768-4679-b626-6d7b07c3ae5f-000000@us-west-2.washingtonpost.com (0101019e18f0ccf6-397742dd-4768-4679-b626-6d7b07c3ae5f-000000@us-west-2.washingtonpost.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Alert from The Washington Post

Virginia Democrats argue that the state’s high court improperly overturned a redistricting referendum last week. But some top Democrats express little hope that the appeal will affect this November’s congressional midterms and are pivoting to waging campaigns in the state’s existing districts.

Virginia Democrats make emergency appeal to U.S. Supreme Court to save new House maps that would give party a boost

news_ktn.title: News Alert: Virginia Democrats ask US Supreme Court to let them use new congressional map
news_ktn.author: bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-023c22b150=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com (bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-023c22b150=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Democratic officials in Virginia asked the US Supreme Court on Monday to reinstate a congressional map that would benefit their party ahead of this year’s midterm elections.
 

Monday, May 11, 2026

View in browser 
 

News Alert: Virginia Democrats ask US Supreme Court to let them use new congressional map

Democratic officials in Virginia asked the US Supreme Court on Monday to reinstate a congressional map that would benefit their party ahead of this year’s midterm elections, the latest map drawing appeal to reach the high court amid a flurry of mid-decade redistricting.

The emergency appeal follows a decision from the Virginia state Supreme Court last week that voided Democrats’ attempt to redraw the state’s US House map via an April referendum. Democrats are asking the US Supreme Court to effectively put that order on hold for this year’s midterm election.

Read more

news_ktn.title: News Alert: Supreme Court briefly extends telehealth and mail access for mifepristone as deliberations continue
news_ktn.author: bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-4b4846d777=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com (bounce-cn2-ZH_CNNAlerts_NDBAN05112026c155001-h-4b4846d777=2@response.emailalerts.cnn.com)
news_ktn.summary:
The Supreme Court on Monday extended a short-term order that will allow women to continue to access the abortion pill mifepristone through telehealth visits while the justices deliberate over a high-profile emergency appeal.
 

Monday, May 11, 2026

View in browser 
 

News Alert: Supreme Court briefly extends telehealth and mail access for mifepristone as deliberations continue

The Supreme Court on Monday extended a short-term order that will allow women to continue to access the abortion pill mifepristone through telehealth visits while the justices deliberate over a high-profile emergency appeal.

Justice Samuel Alito issued a temporary order last week that allowed widespread access to the drug while it considered the case. That “administrative” order had been set to expire Monday evening. The new order extends that stay until Thursday at 5 p.m. ET. The order keeps on hold a May 1 decision from the 5th US Circuit Court of Appeals that had abruptly required women to obtain the drug through in-person visits.

Read more

news_ktn.title: Breaking News: Trump seeks to pause federal gas tax as prices soar amid Iran conflict
news_ktn.author: 0100019e1851c902-e3939aee-3b24-432f-a78c-06c4b7937f6f-000000@email.washingtonpost.com (0100019e1851c902-e3939aee-3b24-432f-a78c-06c4b7937f6f-000000@email.washingtonpost.com)
news_ktn.summary:
Alert from The Washington Post

Congress would have to approve the proposal, and passing the savings on to drivers, rather than oil companies, would be difficult to enforce. The plan reflects President Donald Trump’s limited options to address surging gas prices as negotiations to end the war remain stuck with no immediate way to reopen key shipping lanes.

‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ 
Sign up for alerts
 

Breaking News

May 11, 2:33 p.m. EDT
 

Trump seeks to pause federal gas tax as prices soar amid Iran conflict

Congress would have to approve the proposal, and passing the savings on to drivers, rather than oil companies, would be difficult to enforce. The plan reflects President Donald Trump’s limited options to address surging gas prices as negotiations to end the war remain stuck with no immediate way to reopen key shipping lanes.

news_ktn.title: Breaking News: In Trump administration battle over AI, U.S. spy agencies seek more power
news_ktn.author: 0100019e182ac883-f4704f0f-22dc-45d3-b8db-3eb442da44f3-000000@email.washingtonpost.com (0100019e182ac883-f4704f0f-22dc-45d3-b8db-3eb442da44f3-000000@email.washingtonpost.com)
news_ktn.summary:

News Alert: Ceasefire with Iran is on 'massive life support,' Trump says

President Trump said Monday that the monthlong ceasefire between the US and Iran is on “massive life support.”

While Trump said the ceasefire remains in place, he said it is “unbelievably weak.”

Both Iran and the US have fired shots at each other in the Strait of Hormuz since the ceasefire took effect.

news_ktn.summary:
Eighteen passengers from hantavirus-hit cruise ship are being monitored at US medical facilities, officials said at a press briefing Monday, with 16 in Nebraska and two in Georgia
 

Monday, May 11, 2026

View in browser 
 

News Alert: 18 passengers from hantavirus-hit cruise ship being monitored in Nebraska and Georgia

There are currently 18 people being monitored for hantavirus in US medical facilities, officials said at a press briefing Monday. Seventeen are Americans and one is a British dual-national.

Sixteen of those individuals are at the University of Nebraska — one is in the biocontainment unit after testing positive and 15 are in the quarantine unit. Each of those individuals are currently asymptomatic. They’ve received brief medical assessments, with further assessments planned for later in the day after they’ve had the chance to rest.

Two people were transferred to Atlanta’s Emory University for “further assessment and care,” including at least one individual who is experiencing symptoms. The couple is in a biocontainment unit.

“Let me be crystal clear: The risk of hantavirus to the general public remains very, very low,” said Dr. Brian Christine, DHHS assistant secretary for health.

Follow live updates

News Alert: Press dinner gunman pleads not guilty to attempting to assassinate Trump

Cole Tomas Allen, the man accused of rushing past security at the annual White House Correspondents’ Dinner in Washington, DC, last month pleaded not guilty to attempting to assassinate President Donald Trump and other charges.

Allen was arraigned Monday in Washington, DC, federal court.

Prosecutors say Allen on April 26 entered the hotel where the correspondents’ dinner was being held armed with a shotgun while the president and top administration officials were on a different floor.
